data.title: Japan finance minister 'deeply concerned' over Trump tariff impact
data.description:
Key points:
  • US tariffs, market volatility risk hurting economy, Japan finance minister says
  • Japan to closely communicate with US on FX, Kato says
  • No comment on what will be discussed at talks with Bessent
  • Japan's Katsunobu Kato speaks to Reuters before US meetings

Japan is "deeply concerned" about global economic fallout from U.S. President Donald Trump's trade tariffs, Finance Minister Katsunobu Kato said on Thursday in the government's strongest warning yet as the two nations began trade talks.

In an interview with Reuters in Tokyo hours after the talks began in Washington, Kato also voiced concern over recent volatile market moves triggered by Trump's tariff announcements, saying they could hurt Japan's economic recovery.

"The recent U.S. tariff measures affect various industries and heighten uncertainty. We're deeply concerned they could affect Japan's economy, as well as the global economy, through various routes such as trade and financial markets," said Kato, who plans to go to Washington next week for meetings of the International Monetary Fund and the Group of 20.

Kato is also expected to meet separately with Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to continue the talks begun by Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba's top tariff negotiator Ryosei Akazawa. Trump unexpectedly joined those talks, which included Bessent.

"There's a risk of exerting downward pressure on Japan's economy," Kato said of the U.S. tariffs and the ensuing market volatility, in his first media interview since Trump announced "reciprocal" tariffs on many nations on April 2.

Kato said he will "closely communicate" with the U.S. on currency issues, adding Tokyo and Washington have long agreed that excessive volatility and disorderly moves in currency rates are undesirable.

"It's important for currency rates to move stably, reflecting fundamentals. There's no change to the government's concern over currency market developments, including speculative moves," he said. "We need to scrutinise how the U.S. tariff measures affect financial market moves, including currency rates."

NO WORD ON YEN DISCUSSIONS

Kato declined to comment on how any discussions on currencies could unfold once he meets Bessent, saying that doing so now would be premature and cause undue speculation in the market.

"The only thing I can say is that we will actively exchange views based on Japan's basic stance" on currencies, Kato said, when asked about market speculation that Washington could ask Japan to join a coordinated effort to weaken the dollar.

Trump has indicated he wants the trade talks to include his accusations that Japan intentionally weakens the yen to help its exporters and his complaints that Japan does not pay enough to support U.S. troops based in the country. Japan, which denies manipulating its currency, wants to avoid such topics.

Trade negotiator Akazawa, Japan's economic revitalisation minister, said exchange rates did not come up in Wednesday's talks, adding that both sides deferred to an earlier agreement between their leaders that currency matters would be set aside for talks between their finance chiefs.

Akazawa also brushed aside the idea that Washington might seek a coordinated effort to weaken the dollar.

"As it has done in the past, Japan's government could take action in the market if there are speculative moves but it won't do anything beyond that," Akazawa told reporters in Washington after the talks. "Japan isn't manipulating the market to weaken the yen in the first place."

The policy chief of Ishiba's ruling party, Itsunori Onodera, said on Sunday that Japan must strengthen the yen, such as by helping boost the country's industrial competitiveness, to fight inflation.

U.S. MIGHT TARGET BOJ

But even as politicians talk up the yen, the Trump administration could target the Bank of Japan over the currency's weakness, some analysts say.

For more than a decade, the BOJ pursued a radical monetary easing to fight entrenched deflation, even as other big central banks raised interest rates, driving the yen to near three-decade lows against the dollar. And while the BOJ has begun raising rates, it is moving slowly and rates remain extremely low.

Kato stressed that monetary policy decisions fall under the jurisdiction of the BOJ but said the government will deepen dialogue with the central bank on the economy, including the impact of U.S. tariffs.

"There is no change to our expectations the BOJ would guide monetary policy appropriately to achieve its 2% inflation target in a stable and durable manner," he said.

Some Japanese lawmakers and policymakers have said Japan's heavy reliance on global free trade heightens the need to convince the U.S. that tariffs are counterproductive, and reach out to other countries for policy coordination.

In the bilateral trade negotiations, Japan should explain how it can help the U.S. resuscitate its ailing manufacturing sector and narrow the huge U.S. trade deficit, Kato said.

He countered the view that Trump's protectionist policies could create dysfunction in multilateral cooperation, in which the U.S. plays a crucial role.

"Obviously, any country would put its interest first. But countries have made efforts to iron out their differences," Kato said. "Each country has its own situation and views. But it's our job as policymakers to seek a better approach that eventually benefits the U.S., Japan, and the global economy."

data.title: Albania to offer 35.2 mln euro of 2030 T-notes on Apr 23
data.description:
TIRANA (Albania), April 17 (SeeNews) - Albania’s finance ministry will offer 3.5 billion leks ($40 million/35.2 million euro) worth of fixed-rate Treasury notes maturing in 2030 at an auction on April 23, the central bank of Albania said.

The government securities will mature on January 22, 2030, according to the central bank’s calendar of upcoming auctions.

At the last auction for the same issue of Treasury notes held on March 26, the ministry sold 4.025 billion leks worth of government paper, surpassing its 3.5 billion leks offer. Maximum accepted yield came in at 3.35%, lower than the 3.61% yield achieved at the previous auction held February 19.

(1 euro = 99.4 Albanian leks)

data.title: The case for a return to US outperformance
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 down 0.5%
  • Moncler, Hermes dip after results
  • Siemens Energy rallies
  • Eyes on ECB decision
  • Wall Street futures higher

THE CASE FOR A RETURN TO US OUTPERFORMANCE

You may have seen just one or two headlines exploring the end of the period of U.S. exceptionalism which dominated markets for the last few years.

Certainly there's some serious rebalancing going on, but Citi FX analysts have a note out offering the case that the move out of U.S. assets is temporary not permanent.

First, they say, in the past 30 years, the only relatively long-lasting period of rest of the world earnings outperformance was during the middle of the first decade of this century, a result of China's entry into the WTO and the collapse in European peripheral bond yields after the creation of the euro.

And while Europe will spend more on defence in the coming months, and China's going to do a bit of stimulus, Citi says that neither compares to the situation 20 or so years ago.

On the other hand they say "the U.S. continues to have better demographics, rule of law, a consumer that remains resilient through numerous “once in a lifetime” crises (GFC, COVID, etc), and a more insulated economy."

And on top of that they expect tariffs and trade negotiations to become a smaller focal point for markets, with tax cuts and deregulation supporting growth in 2026.

As a result "this could lead to cyclical return towards US assets and the dollar" they say, adding they expect euro/dollar to peak this year rather than continue its passage higher on a sustained multi-year trend.

(Alun John)

*****

THURSDAY'S OTHER LIVE MARKETS POSTS:

EUROPE BEFORE THE BELL: CAUTIOUS START BEFORE ECB CLICK HERE

data.title: Berenberg Cuts LVMH Price Target on Weak Chinese Demand, Macro Uncertainty
data.description:
Berenberg lowered its price target on LVMH to 570 euros from 740 euros, citing a "material miss" in the French company's first-quarter revenue and the expected impact of the current economic uncertainty on the luxury sector.

The research firm reiterated its buy rating on the stock.

LVMH's first-quarter revenue fell 3% organically, compared with the consensus forecast of a 1% rise. Berenberg attributed the decline to reduced Chinese consumption, particularly offshore spending in markets such as Japan.

"Despite our bearish stance, Chinese consumers have proven to be even weaker than we had assumed. With erratic US policymaking adding to the economic uncertainty, we now assume LVMH, and the rest of the luxury industry, will struggle to grow revenues until well into 2026," analysts wrote.

Berenberg also "materially" trimmed its revenue forecasts for LVMH by 5% for 2025 and 7% for 2026, alongside EPS cuts of 15% and 19%.

data.title: China Warns Automakers on Potentially Misleading Advertising for Driving Tech
data.description:
By Jiahui Huang

China warned automakers not to exaggerate the capabilities of autonomous-driving technology integrated into their cars.

The country's Ministry of Industry and Information Technology said in a statement late Wednesday that it held a meeting with nearly 60 industry representatives.

Automakers should vigorously test their autonomous-driving systems and clarify the limits of the technology as well as the safety measures in place, according to the statement.

Car companies shouldn't exaggerate the systems' capabilities or engage in false marketing, the ministry said.

The move came after a fatal car accident involving a Xiaomi electric vehicle in China late last month, which prompted industry analysts and the public to raise concerns about the safety of advanced driver-assistance systems.

The fatal incident attracted public attention and was a trending topic on Chinese social media. Users on social-media sites expressed concern about the safety of advanced driving-assistance systems, which are widely used in EVs in China and have became one of the selling points for many brands.

Some analysts have expressed concern about potentially misleading promotional material used to market autonomous-driving features.

Most Chinese carmakers offer level 2 autonomous-driving features, which still require a human driver to hold on to the steering wheel while in motion and to remain alert to surroundings. However, analysts note that marketing materials could mislead consumers to think that the cars can perform all driving tasks without need for any human intervention.

Chinese auto companies have rushed to launch EVs equipped with autonomous-driving features, especially after EV giant BYD in February rolled out the technology to its mass-market models.

Write to Jiahui Huang at jiahui.huang@wsj.com

data.title: Marex Group plc Announces Pricing of the Public Offering
data.description:
NEW YORK, April 17, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Marex Group plc (“Marex”) , the diversified global financial services platform, today announces the pricing of the public offering (the “Offering”) of 10,283,802 ordinary shares by certain selling shareholders (the “Selling Shareholders”) at $35.50 per share. In connection with the Offering, the Selling Shareholders have granted the underwriters a 30-day option to purchase up to an additional 1,542,570 ordinary shares.

Marex is not selling any ordinary shares in the Offering and will not receive any proceeds from any sale of shares by the Selling Shareholders. The Offering is expected to close on April 17, 2025, subject to customary closing conditions.

Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC, Barclays and Jefferies are acting as lead book-running managers and as representatives of the underwriters for the Offering. UBS Investment Bank, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods, a Stifel Company, Piper Sandler & Co., TD Securities and Berenberg are acting as bookrunners for the Offering.

The Offering is being made only by means of a prospectus. Copies of the prospectus relating to the Offering may be obtained from:

  • Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC, Attention: Prospectus Department, 200 West Street, New York, New York 10282, via telephone: 1-866-471-2526, or via email: prospectus-ny@ny.email.gs.com;
  • Barclays Capital Inc., c/o Broadridge Financial Solutions, 1155 Long Island Avenue, Edgewood, NY 11717, by telephone at 1-888-603-5847, or by email at barclaysprospectus@broadridge.com; or
  • Jefferies LLC, Attention: Equity Syndicate Prospectus Department, 520 Madison Avenue, New York, NY 10022, by phone at (877) 821-7388, or by email at Prospectus_Department@Jefferies.com.

A registration statement on Form F-1 relating to the Offering has been filed with, and was declared effective by, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (the “SEC”). This press release does not constitute an offer to sell or the solicitation of an offer to buy any securities, and shall not constitute an offer, solicitation or sale in any jurisdiction in which such offer, solicitation or sale would be unlawful prior to registration or qualification under the securities laws of that jurisdiction.

Forward Looking Statements

This release contains forward-looking statements within the meaning of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. All statements contained in this release that do not relate to matters of historical fact should be considered forward-looking statements, including the expected closing date of the Offering. In some cases, these forward-looking statements can be identified by words or phrases such as “may,” “will,” “expect,” “anticipate,” “aim,” “estimate,” “intend,” “plan,” “believe,” “potential,” “continue,” “is/are likely to” or other similar expressions. These forward-looking statements are subject to risks, uncertainties and assumptions, some of which are beyond our control. In addition, these forward-looking statements reflect our current views with respect to future events and are not a guarantee of future performance. Actual outcomes may differ materially from the information contained in the forward-looking statements as a result of a number of factors, including, without limitation: subdued commodity market activity or pricing levels; the effects of geopolitical events, terrorism and wars, such as the effect of Russia’s military action in Ukraine or the on-going conflicts in the Middle East, on market volatility, global macroeconomic conditions and commodity prices; changes in interest rate levels; the risk of our clients and their related financial institutions defaulting on their obligations to us; regulatory, reputational and financial risks as a result of our international operations; software or systems failure, loss or disruption of data or data security failures; an inability to adequately hedge our positions and limitations on our ability to modify contracts and the contractual protections that may be available to us in OTC derivatives transactions; market volatility, reputational risk and regulatory uncertainty related to commodity markets, equities, fixed income, foreign exchange; the impact of climate change and the transition to a lower carbon economy on supply chains and the size of the market for certain of our energy products; the impact of changes in judgments, estimates and assumptions made by management in the application of our accounting policies on our reported financial condition and results of operations; lack of sufficient financial liquidity; if we fail to comply with applicable law and regulation, we may be subject to enforcement or other action, forced to cease providing certain services or obliged to change the scope or nature of our operations; significant costs, including adverse impacts on our business, financial condition and results of operations, and expenses associated with compliance with relevant regulations; and if we fail to remediate the material weaknesses we identified in our internal control over financial reporting or prevent material weaknesses in the future, the accuracy and timing of our financial statements may be impacted, which could result in material misstatements in our financial statements or failure to meet our reporting obligations and subject us to potential delisting, regulatory investments or civil or criminal sanctions, and other risks discussed under the caption “Risk Factors” in our Registration Statement filed on Form F-1 with the SEC on April 14, 2025 and our other reports filed with the SEC.

The forward-looking statements made in this release relate only to events or information as of the date on which the statements are made in this release. Except as required by law, we undertake no obligation to update or revise publicly any forward-looking statements, whether as a result of new information, future events or otherwise, after the date on which the statements are made or to reflect the occurrence of unanticipated events.

data.title: Ford to recall more than 123,000 vehicles, NHTSA says
data.description:
Ford Motor is recalling 123,611 vehicles in the U.S. due to a fluid leak that could reduce braking performance and increase stopping distance, the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration said on Thursday.

The recall affects certain 2017–18 Ford F-150, Expedition, and Lincoln Navigator models, the regulator said, adding that a faulty part could cause brake fluid to leak, which may reduce braking power and make it harder to stop the vehicles.

data.title: Shurgard Self Storage Buys New Property in the Netherlands
data.description:
Shurgard Self Storage entered a conditional purchase deal for a new property in The Hague, the Netherlands.

The company said in a statement dated Wednesday and filed with the exchange Thursday that the location will be developed into a new 6,500-square-meter freehold self-storage facility, which will open in 2026.

The Euronext Brussels-listed self-storage company hopes to secure a building permit in the following months, allowing it to begin construction work.

data.title: Market Chatter: Samsung Electro-Mechanics Secures MLCC Supply Deal with BYD
data.description:
Samsung Electro-Mechanics began mass shipments of multilayer ceramic capacitors (MLCCs) to Chinese automaker BYD , after securing final approval.

With BYD targeting 5.5 million electric vehicle units in 2025, Samsung's MLCC supply could surpass tens of billions of units, mainly for use in high-reliability automotive systems like advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS), Pulse News reported Wednesday.

The agreement follows Samsung Chairman Jay Y. Lee's March visit to China, where he met BYD executives in Shenzhen, the report said.

Shares of BYD rose nearly 1% at market close on the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong while those listed on the Shenzhen Stock Exchange declined about 1%.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: Aluminum Holds Below $2,400 Threshold
data.description:
Aluminum futures remained below the $2,400 per tonne mark, maintaining most of the selloff that bottomed at the eight-month low of $2,340 on April 9th, tracking the bearish momentum for base metals in international exchanges as the trade war between the US and China hurt the outlook on global manufacturing.

After an exchange of tariff increases, China settled levies on the US at 125% and added a batch of companies to its export-restriction list, while the US raised tariffs to 145% and threatened additional barriers on selected metals.

The magnitude of manufacturing growth concerns were large enough to offset supply concerns in the US, as previous tariffs on aluminum by Trump were set to stress domestic capacity.

Supply is also due to slow in China as the current production pace is close to the output cap of 45 million tons, initially mandated to aid carbon emission targets.

data.title: SocGen's Overnight Economic News Summary
data.description:
Societe Generale in its early Thursday economic news summary pointed out:

— US dollar broadly bid, 10-year United States Treasury +3bps at 4.30%. Federal Reserve Chair Powell: waiting for more data on the economy's direction before changing interest rates, tariff policies risk pushing inflation and employment further from the central bank's goals.

— Day ahead: Italy Prime Minister Meloni meets United States President Trump on tariffs. European Central Bank forecast to cut 25bps. U.S. weekly jobless claims. Philly Fed business outlook. Fed speaker Barr. Bank of England credit conditions survey. Turkey's central bank forecast to leave the key rate at 42.50%.

— China and Japan raised UST holdings by $23.5 billion and $46.6 billion, respectively, in February, Canada bought $55.3 billion, Belgium (Euroclear) $17.3 billion. 20-year UST auction solid, stop through 0.4bp, b/c 2.57x, non-dealers 83%, indirects 70.7%.

— Japan: investors sold 512 billion yen of international bonds last week, marking the sixth straight week of outflow. President Trump touts 'big progress' in U.S.-Japan trade talks, foreign exchange not discussed, according to trade negotiator Akazawa.

— Bank of Korea leaves key rate unchanged at 2.75%, sees significant downside risks to growth due to U.S. tariffs, 2025 gross domestic product likely to be below February forecast of 1.5%. +0.3% at 1,420.

— Nikkei +1.35%, EUR 10-year IRS unchanged at 2.52%, Brent crude +0.6% at $66.25/barrel, Gold +0.1% at $3,329/oz.

data.title: Regulatory snag in BNP’s AXA IM bid may cast doubt on similar deals, sources say
data.description:
Key points:
  • ECB decision affects BNP Paribas' AXA IM acquisition returns
  • Dealmakers expected ECB support for asset management consolidation
  • BNP still benefits from parts of the Danish Compromise, consultant and analyst say

BNP Paribas embarked on a 5.1 billion euro ($5.8 billion) purchase of an asset management business last year expecting regulators would let it through with minimal impact on the bank's capital, people familiar with the matter said, a model some peers were keen to replicate.

But the latest assessment from its chief supervisor, the European Central Bank, tempered those expectations, leading to a greater hit to its capital than the lender had initially priced in, the people said.

BNP said on Monday it had lowered its expected returns on the deal following guidance from the ECB on the so-called Danish Compromise - a prudential treatment that allows lower capital requirements for banks that own insurance units.

It added that the prudential treatment of the deal will be disclosed at closing, pending final talks with regulators.

BNP and the ECB declined to comment.

Dealmakers had anticipated a wave of tie-ups after the BNP-AXA deal as European money managers sought to fend off U.S. rivals and demand grows for cheap technology-driven investing. Some analysts had foreseen other banks would follow the French lender's footsteps as a way to boost fees.

"It will maybe dampen excitement a little bit," said Johann Scholtz, an analyst at Morningstar. "What makes it a little bit surprising is, in the current environment, you'd expect the ECB to be maybe a little bit more supportive of consolidation across the board."

RAISING THE BAR

In an interview published on Friday on the ECB's website, Claudia Buch, the central bank’s chief supervisor, said the central bank interprets the “Danish Compromise” as applying to the insurance sector — not to asset management firms.

The ECB's decision took some dealmakers by surprise and the lack of clarity around the regulatory treatment may make such transactions more difficult to execute, several said.

Some said parties pursuing deals may now seek written assurances from the ECB, effectively raising the bar for such transactions.

Not all experts shared that view.

"I do not believe that this will materially impact similar activity in the EU," said Alvin Abraham, chief executive of prudential risk consultancy firm Katalysys.

The ECB's decision on the use of the Danish Compromise, by limiting the impact on its capital reserves, signals that many of the benefits of the EU provision still apply, Abraham added.

Matthew Clark, an analyst at Mediobanca, agreed that BNP's statement on Monday indicated a partial application of the favourable capital treatment.

"That very strongly points to the idea that there's been some kind of splitting mechanism used whereby the ECB said: 'you're buying an asset manager where a lot of the business is insurance related, so let's split that kind of notionally in two," Clark said, meaning that the Danish Compromise was applied to the insurance part AXA IM's activities only.

The Danish Compromise is a provision that makes it less costly for banks to hold stakes in insurance companies. Instead of subtracting the full value of those holdings from their capital, banks can apply a risk-based calculation.

By lowering the capital impact of insurance stakes, the measure encourages banks to own insurers and can reduce the cost of certain acquisitions made through insurance arms.

BNP is acquiring AXA IM through its insurance business Cardif.

However, following the ECB's comments on the "prudential" treatment of the acquisition of asset management companies, the French lender cut returns guidance to 14% in the third year, from a previously projected 18%, followed by more than 20% in the fourth year, it said in its statement on Monday.

BNP's market update follows a similar ECB assessment last month, when the regulator issued a negative opinion on Italian bank Banco BPM's request for favourable capital treatment for its proposed bid for fund manager Anima.

BNP Paribas shares have gained about 15% since the AXA IM deal was announced in early August, trailing French rivals Crédit Agricole and Societe Generale, as well as the European banking index (.SX7E), which is up by close to 28% over the same period.

($1 = 0.8814 euros)

data.title: Mongolian Mining Sees Steady Coal Output But Lower Sales in Q1
data.description:
Mongolian Mining reported a 3% yearly rise in raw coal production to 3.67 million tonnes in Q1, though output remained flat compared to the previous quarter, according to its Wednesday bourse filing.

The company processed 3.75 million tonnes into 2.11 million tonnes of washed coking coal, marking a slight 2% annual decline but a modest quarterly improvement.

Sales of premium coal products fell to 1.6 million tonnes, down 3% from last year and 15% from Q4 2024, with hard coking coal making up the bulk of shipments.

data.title: Renault chairman Senard to step down from Nissan's board
data.description: Renault Chairman Jean-Dominique Senard is to step down from Nissan's board of directors, the Japanese automaker said on Thursday, marking another high-profile departure at the company as it battles to reverse declining performance.

data.title: Nvidia CEO Visits Beijing After Latest China Export Curb
data.description:
By Sherry Qin

Nvidia's chief executive on Thursday met with the head of a Chinese trade body during a visit to Beijing, Chinese state media reported, days after Washington set new export curbs on chips the company exports to China.

State broadcaster CCTV said in a report that Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang held talks with Ren Hongbin, president of the China Council for the Promotion of International Trade, a trade agency under the Ministry of Commerce.

According to the reports, Huang said that China is a very important market for Nvidia and that he hopes to continue cooperating with China.

CCTV said Huang arrived in Beijing on Thursday under the invitation of the state-backed trade group headed by Ren, a former deputy commerce minister.

Nvidia didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

Earlier this week, the U.S. government said Nvidia will now need a license to export its H20 processors to China and several other countries, saying the measure will be in place "for the indefinite future." The graphics-chip maker said it could book a charge of up to $5.5 billion related to the controls.

The U.S. banned exports of the most advanced semiconductors and chip-manufacturing equipment to China during the Biden administration. The H20 processor is a less-powerful version designed to comply with U.S. export controls, especially for the Chinese market.

Chinese companies, including tech giants Tencent, Alibaba and ByteDance, buy Nvidia's chips to advance AI development. Analysts say Chinese tech companies have stepped up H20 orders to avoid supply disruptions and keep pursuing AI breakthroughs.

Write to Sherry Qin at sherry.qin@wsj.com

data.title: Kremlin calls Qatari emir's visit very important, says he and Putin will sign documents
data.description: The Kremlin said that Russian President Vladimir Putin and Qatari Emir Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al-Thani will sign some documents during the latter's visit to Russia on Thursday, a trip that it said was "very important".

data.title: China Remains Open to Dialogue With U.S., Commerce Ministry Says
data.description:
China's Ministry of Commerce said Thursday it has maintained working-level communication with its U.S. counterparts and remains open to dialogue based on "mutual respect."

At a weekly press briefing, ministry spokesperson He Yongqian answered questions on U.S. President Trump's recent comments asking China to reach out to him for a trade deal.

She said that unilateral tariff hikes were initiated entirely by the U.S., saying, "whoever tied the knot should be the one to untie it."

"We urge the U.S. to immediately stop its campaign of maximum pressure and end its coercion and blackmail," she said in the briefing.

"Disputes should be resolved through equal dialogue based on mutual respect," she said.

Write to Singapore Editors at singaporeeditors@dowjones.com
